text: The pump capacity of the heart is the primary factor limiting maximal oxygen uptake during large muscle mass exercise, rather than peripheral muscle adaptations.
The capillary bed of skeletal muscle and mitochondrial volume can be enhanced with endurance training, contributing to improved oxygen utilization.
The upper limit of human oxygen transport capacity appears to be in the range of 80-90 ml/kg/min.

When expressed per kilogram of active muscle, maximal oxygen consumption capacity is approximately 150-200 ml/kg muscle/min, which can be consumed by only a fraction of total muscle mass during intense contraction.
Peripheral muscle adaptations (capillary density and mitochondrial volume) are necessary but not sufficient for elevating VO2max; central cardiovascular factors (heart and lungs) set the upper limit.
